title: Strengthen Wood Product Supply Chains Act of 2025
short_title: Strengthen Wood Product Supply Chains Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes changes to a law called the Lacey Act that controls imports of fish, wildlife, and plant products. When government workers want to check these imported goods, they will now have to follow stricter time limits and give better notice to the people who own the goods. The bill gives importers more rights to move their products, get test results, and ask for reviews if they think the government made a mistake. If importers believe their goods were taken unfairly, they will have more options to fight back in court.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Strengthen Wood Product Supply Chains Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Fair Enforcement of Lacey Act Amendments of 1981
sections.text: This section changes the Lacey Act Amendments of nineteen eighty one. The Lacey Act is a law that controls the import and export of fish, wildlife, and plants to prevent illegal trade. The changes create new rules for when government officials stop and hold imported goods for inspection. When officials detain merchandise (goods being imported), they must give the importer a notice within five days or release the goods. The notice must explain why the goods were stopped, how long they will be held, what tests will be done, and what information might speed up the process. Within ten days of giving the notice, officials must let the importer move the detained goods to another location if the importer pays storage fees and provides a bond for security, unless moving the goods would hurt the purpose of the law. If officials test the goods, they must share the test results and give enough information for the importer to repeat the tests themselves. Within thirty days, officials must either release the goods or officially seize them. If they do not act within thirty days, the law considers this an automatic seizure. Importers can ask for an administrative review of any seizure within thirty days. If the review does not go in their favor or if officials do not respond in time, importers can file a claim in federal court. In court cases, judges should help the importer unless the government can prove with strong evidence that they had good reasons for not making a decision about the goods. The section defines detained merchandise as goods stopped for inspection and merchandise as any imported fish, wildlife, or plants meant for sale.
